context_update.summary: This front-matter chunk introduces the paper, providing the full title, authors, keywords, and the abstract. The investigated material is an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C (wt.%) low-density steel free from ordered κ‑carbides, with an ~8.5% density reduction. One sample was hydrogen-charged to a concentration of 101 mass ppm (confirmed by thermal desorption, Figure 1). Room-temperature tensile tests reveal a 10% increase in yield stress and ~8% increase in elongation to fracture compared to an uncharged reference, indicating high hydrogen embrittlement resistance. Hydrogen induces two key deformation mechanisms: hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding (HEDB), which causes macroscopic kink bands and strain localization, and hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB), which creates sub‑micron strain gradients and localized shear at cell blocks. HEMB is found to promote plastic relaxation and increased storage of geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs), mitigating damage and boosting strain‑hardening capacity. The deformation structure will be analyzed by ECCI, EBSD, and STEM. Subsequent chunks will need to track these mechanisms, microstructural features (cell blocks, kink bands, microbands), and property data for the hydrogen-charged and reference samples.
processing_route: hydrogen charging to 101 mass ppm H
room temperature tensile deformation
mechanisms: hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding (HEDB)
hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB)
plastic relaxation and enhanced GND storage

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the end of the introduction and the full Materials and Methods section. The investigated material is an annealed, fully austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C (wt.%) low-density steel with a 175 μm grain size, free of κ‑carbides. Cylindrical specimens (6 mm diameter, 30 mm gauge) were machined along the rolling direction, mirror polished, and tested in tension at 5.0×10⁻⁵ s⁻¹ at 295 K. Hydrogen charging was performed at 100 MPa H₂ and 543 K for 200 h, resulting in 101 mass ppm diffusible hydrogen (TDA peak at ~520 K, Fig. 1); the uncharged reference contains <5 mass ppm. Mechanical properties (Table 1, Fig. 2) show a hydrogen-induced increase in YS (+27 MPa), UTS (+15 MPa), TFS (+20 MPa), UEL (+0.03), and ETF (+0.04). Deformation structure analysis uses ECCI, EBSD (20 kV, step 50–100 nm) and STEM (200 kV, FIB thinned at 5 kV/200 pA). ECCI samples were polished to 250 nm colloidal silica. Grain orientations from IPFs (Fig. 3) are classified into Type 1a, Type 1b, Type 2, and Type 3 dislocation structures; the uncharged sample’s Type 1a and 1b are characterized by GNBs and IDBs (Fig. 4). Later chunks should track these structure types, the hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding/microbanding mechanisms, and their correlation to the measured property changes.
processing_route: annealed to produce 175 μm fully austenitic grains
machining along rolling direction (6 mm diameter, 30 mm gauge)
mirror polishing (1 μm diamond suspension)
hydrogen charging: 100 MPa H₂, 543 K, 200 h
room temperature tensile testing at 5.0×10⁻⁵ s⁻¹ in air
key_variables: hydrogen concentration 101 mass ppm vs <5 mass ppm
EBSD: 20 kV, WD 15–20 mm, step 50–100 nm
ECCI: 15–20 kV, BSE detector, WD 6–7 mm, high-current mode
STEM: 200 kV, FIB final thinning 5 kV, 200 pA
microstructure: as-annealed: fully austenitic, grain size 175 μm, no κ-carbides
undeformed reference: dislocation structures classified into Type 1a (GNBs, IDBs) and Type 1b
properties: uncharged: YS 275±10 MPa, UTS 580±5 MPa, TFS 825±15 MPa, UEL 0.43±0.01, ETF 0.51±0.01
hydrogen-charged: YS 302±10 MPa, UTS 595±10 MPa, TFS 845±15 MPa, UEL 0.46±0.02, ETF 0.55±0.01
mechanisms: hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding (HEDB) and hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB) introduced in introduction, detailed mechanisms to be discussed later
figures_or_tables: Fig. 1 (TDA hydrogen desorption curve, one lattice diffusion peak)
Fig. 2 (engineering and true stress-strain, strain-hardening curves)
Fig. 3 (inverse pole figures for grain orientation classification)
Fig. 4 (Type 1a and 1b dislocation structures in uncharged sample)

context_update.summary: Current chunk details the hydrogen charging procedure (100 MPa H₂, 543 K, 200 h) that yields a uniform 101 mass ppm H (TDA with single lattice-diffusion peak, Fig. 1), compared to <5 mass ppm in the uncharged sample. Room-temperature tensile tests (Fig. 2, Table 1) show hydrogen increases YS by ~27 MPa, flow stress by 14–25 MPa, UEL from 0.43 to 0.46, and ETF from 0.51 to 0.55. The strain-hardening curve for both conditions exhibits four stages (A–D); no twinning-induced hardening stage is observed, consistent with a high SFE (64±10 mJ/m²) that postpones deformation twinning. The dislocation structures in the uncharged sample at ε=0.4 are classified based on grain orientation (Figs. 3–5): Type 1a/1b cell blocks (GNBs/IDBs) along [112]–[101]//TA, Type 2 dislocation cells near [001]//TA, and Type 3 mixed dislocation cells + microbands (150–200 nm width) + dense dislocation walls near [111]//TA. A combined ECCI/EBSD approach (Fig. 6) is introduced to quantify hydrogen effects on these structures. The next sections should address hydrogen-induced changes in the dislocation structure types, boundary misorientations, and the associated HEDB/HEMB mechanisms that explain the enhanced ductility.
processing_route: hydrogen charging: 100 MPa H₂, 543 K, 200 h → 101 mass ppm H
tensile test at 295 K, strain rate 5×10⁻⁵ s⁻¹
deformation to ε=0.4 for microstructure observations
microstructure: Type 1: cell blocks with GNBs/IDBs along [112]–[101]//TA
Type 1a: primary GNB1 + secondary GNB2 + IDBs
Type 1b: two GNB sets (GNB1, GNB2)
Type 2: dislocation cells (IDBs) near [001]//TA, octahedral slip
Type 3: homogeneous DCs + inhomogeneous MBs (150–200 nm) + DDWs near [111]//TA
properties: uncharged YS 275±10 MPa, UTS 580±5 MPa, TFS 825±15 MPa, UEL 0.43±0.01, ETF 0.51±0.01
hydrogen-charged YS 302±10 MPa, UTS 595±10 MPa, TFS 845±15 MPa, UEL 0.46±0.02, ETF 0.55±0.01
similar strain-hardening curves, stages A–D, no extra twinning stage
mechanisms: high SFE postpones deformation twinning
hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding and microbanding (to be detailed in subsequent sections)

Current chunk details the hydrogen charging procedure (100 MPa H₂, 543 K, 200 h) that yields a uniform 101 mass ppm H (TDA with single lattice-diffusion peak, Fig. 1), compared to <5 mass ppm in the uncharged sample. Room-temperature tensile tests (Fig. 2, Table 1) show hydrogen increases YS by ~27 MPa, flow stress by 14–25 MPa, UEL from 0.43 to 0.46, and ETF from 0.51 to 0.55. context_update.summary: Current chunk quantitatively analyzes the influence of hydrogen (101 mass ppm) on Type 1 dislocation structures (cell blocks) in Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C steel using ECCI and EBSD. Hydrogen does not change the morphology of Type 1a/1b cell blocks but significantly increases average IDB misorientation by ~0.25° and average GNB misorientation by ~1° (Fig. 7). The GNB misorientation distribution is broadened, with a higher fraction of boundaries exceeding 2° and extending up to ~7-8°. Detailed analysis of Type 1a CBs (Fig. 8) shows GNBs are formed by DDWs of thickness 90±10 nm, and localized microbands (MBs) with thickness 150-200 nm and length 1.5-2.0 µm nucleate along GNB sections. KAM-EBSD reveals DDW misorientations ~1° and MB misorientations ~2°, establishing that boundaries <2° correspond to DDWs and >2° to MBs. In Type 1b CBs, MBs are longer (~3-4 µm) with higher misorientations (~5°). The hydrogen-enhanced population of high-angle GNBs (>2°) is linked to enhanced MB nucleation, which BF-STEM confirms as paired dislocation sheets; no κ-carbides are detected (Fig. 9). This establishes the hydrogen-induced modification of GNB boundary character and localized MB nucleation, which should be connected to Type 2 and 3 structures and the overall HEDB/HEMB mechanism in subsequent chunks.
processing_route: room temperature tensile deformation to ε=0.4
microstructure: Type 1a cell blocks: single GNB system, DDWs, localized MBs
Type 1b cell blocks: two GNB systems, longer MBs with higher misorientation
MBs: lenticular, 150-200 nm thick, paired dislocation sheets (BF-STEM)
No ordered κ-carbides detected
mechanisms: Hydrogen-enhanced localized MB nucleation along GNBs
MB nucleation increases average GNB misorientation
Hydrogen-induced effect on plasticity

context_update.summary: This chunk covers Type 2 and Type 3 dislocation structures in hydrogen-charged Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C steel. Type 2: hydrogen induces macroscopic deformation bands (DBs, 10–30 μm) identified as kink bands; inside DB1 ([2 1 15]//TA), MBs (150–200 nm, spacing ~2–3 μm) and DCs appear, with DC size refined from 220±10 nm to 192±10 nm (Fig. 11). Type 3: in [5 4 6]//TA grains, DDW/DC structures coexist with DBs (0.5–1.0 μm) and MBs (~200 nm, 2–3 μm spacing) on (-1 1 1) secondary planes. BF-STEM shows DBs cause larger shear offsets (75–100 nm) on DDWs and are coarser (0.5–1.0 μm vs 0.3–0.5 μm) with merging tendency. No extra diffraction spots confirm plasticity-driven localization. Inside DBs, high dislocation density (~1.3×10^14 m^−2), dissolved DDWs, Lomer-Cottrell dislocations, jogs, and cross-slip are observed. Type 3 DC size refines from 236±10 nm to 212±10 nm, but misorientation is largely unchanged (~0.87–0.90°). Relevant figures: 10–16. Future chunks should connect these findings to the HEDB/HEMB mechanisms and HE resistance.

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the effect of hydrogen on Type 3 dislocation cell size and misorientation, revealing a refinement from 236±10 nm to 212±10 nm and negligible change in misorientation (0.87°→0.90°). Fractography shows fully ductile fracture for both uncharged and hydrogen-charged samples; hydrogen-charged dimples are finer (1.5–3.0 μm) due to hydrogen-enhanced strain localization increasing void nucleation. Discussion (4.1) reports that 101 mass ppm H increases yield stress by 27 MPa and unusually enhances ductility. Section 4.2 compares homogeneous plasticity structures: hydrogen has a small effect on DCs (~10% refinement) but enhances DDWs and strongly promotes inhomogeneous plasticity (DBs/MBs), termed HEDB and HEMB. Mechanism: hydrogen shielding stabilizes GNBs via reduced Peach-Kohler force; dislocation trapping by IDBs is limited. SAD confirms plasticity-driven nature. Key figures: Fig. 16 (DC size/misorientation), Fig. 17 (fracture), Fig. 12 (Type 3 DBs/MBs). Future chunks should connect HEDB/HEMB to HE resistance and possibly compare with prior Type 1 and 2 structures.

context_update.summary: This chunk discusses hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding (HEDB) and hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB) in the Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C steel. HEDB produces macroscopic kink-bands (10–30 μm) near <001>//TA and submicron DBs near <111>//TA; hydrogen intensifies lattice rotations and generates high secondary dislocation density (~1–3×10¹⁴ m⁻²) within DBs. Both uncharged and charged samples show DBs (Fig. 13), confirming hydrogen's non-geometric role. HEMB occurs at GNB structures via boundary splitting (GNB-assisted MB nucleation) and inside DBs via massive secondary glide and cross-slip (DB-assisted MB nucleation). EBSD shows larger lattice rotations in charged GNB-assisted MBs (2–8° vs 2–3°), attributed to enhanced GND storage. BF-STEM reveals dislocation sheets evolving into MBs. A critical cross-slip stress of 350–520 MPa exceeds the applied resolved stress ~330 MPa, implying DB internal stress promotes cross-slip, contrary to predicted suppression. Key figures: 7, 8, 9, 10, 12, 13, 14, 15, 18. Future sections will link HEMB to HE resistance.

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the BF-STEM observation of dislocation structures inside DBs, the effect of HEMB on HE resistance (Section 4.3), a comparative HE resistance analysis (Section 4.4), and the conclusions (Section 5). HEMB is shown to promote plastic accommodation, relax DB internal stress, store GNDs, mitigate grain boundary damage, and enhance strain-hardening, leading to high HE resistance. Quantitative data include Fig. 16 showing hydrogen-induced shifts in type 3 cell misorientation (0.5° fraction 0.08→0.13, 1.5° 0.05→0.11) and size (200 nm fraction 0.16→0.30). Fractography (Fig. 17) confirms hydrogen refines dimple size without changing the ductile fracture mode. The Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C steel with 101 mass ppm H achieves IHE = -0.045, yield stress increase ~10%, elongation increase ~8%, and UTS ~580 MPa, placing it in the high-HE-resistance region comparable to 310S and CoNiV alloys (Fig. 19, Table 2). Conclusions summarize cell size refinement ~10%, enhanced DDW formation, HEDB and HEMB definitions, and their contribution to HE resistance. End of paper; no subsequent chunks expected.

Key variables: 101 mass ppm hydrogen; yield stress increase 10%; elongation to fracture increase ~8%; HEDB; HEMB; initial strain rate 5.0×10⁻⁵ s⁻¹; hydrogen concentration 101 mass ppm vs <5 mass ppm; EBSD: 20 kV, WD 15–20 mm, step 50–100 nm
Microstructure: as-annealed: fully austenitic, grain size 175 μm, no κ-carbides; undeformed reference: dislocation structures classified into Type 1a (GNBs, IDBs) and Type 1b; Type 1: cell blocks with GNBs/IDBs along [112]–[101]//TA; Type 1a: primary GNB1 + secondary GNB2 + IDBs; Type 1b: two GNB sets (GNB1, GNB2); Type 2: dislocation cells (IDBs) near [001]//TA, octahedral slip; Type 3: homogeneous DCs + inhomogeneous MBs (150–200 nm) + DDWs near [111]//TA; Type 1a cell blocks: single GNB system, DDWs, localized MBs
